reforms.description: The Budget for 1989 introduced a £10 million bank-funded loans scheme aimed at supporting young entrepreneurs in starting their businesses, alongside the establishment of the International Financial Service Centre (IFSC) in Dublin, which aims to attract foreign financial institutions by offering various fiscal incentives, including a significantly reduced corporation tax rate and exemptions from local taxes for ten years.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These initiatives are designed to foster entrepreneurship and attract foreign investment, which are expected to enhance economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The Budget for 1989 and the establishment of the IFSC have been put into action.
reforms.importance_rationale: Both initiatives represent significant structural changes aimed at boosting the startup ecosystem and attracting foreign investment.
reforms.source_quote: An important recent development is the Government-inspired establishment of an off-shore financial centre. The International Financial Service Centre (IFSC) in the Custom House Docks Area in Dublin will benefit from various fiscal incentives for trading in non-Irish currencies.

reforms.description: The National Development Plan 1989-1993 outlines a £9.1 billion spending programme aimed at creating new jobs and enhancing economic growth through various structural measures, integrating efforts to increase total investment in key sectors and infrastructure.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The plan is designed to stimulate job creation and economic growth through significant investment in various sectors.
reforms.status_evidence: The National Development Plan has been outlined and is set to be implemented over the specified period.
reforms.importance_rationale: This plan represents a major structural initiative aimed at transforming the economy and enhancing growth.
reforms.source_quote: The Government announces the National Development Plan 1989-1993, setting out structural measures which Ireland proposes to implement.

reforms.description: The Irish government has established major incentives programs, including grants, training programs, company tax relief, and accelerated depreciation allowances, to attract export-oriented foreign companies.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These incentives are aimed at attracting foreign companies, which can lead to increased investment and job creation.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has actively established these programs to support foreign investment.
reforms.importance_rationale: The establishment of these incentive programs represents a significant policy shift to enhance the attractiveness of Ireland for foreign investment.
reforms.source_quote: Major incentives programmes, consisting of grants, training programmes, company tax relief, and accelerated depreciation allowances have been established in the past.

reforms.description: The recent reduction in the scale of investment subsidies and a reorientation of aid to industry towards marketing and technology transfer is a step in the right direction.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: While the reorientation may improve efficiency in aid distribution, the reduction in subsidies could hinder some investment activities.
reforms.status_evidence: The government has enacted this reorientation of aid to industry.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant adjustment in the approach to investment subsidies, though not as transformative as other reforms.
reforms.source_quote: The recent reduction in the scale of investment subsidies, and a reorientation of aid to industry towards marketing and technology transfer is a step in the right direction.
